coredata.dc:title: Functionalized carbon dots composite cation exchange membranes: Improved electrochemical performance and salt removal efficiency
coredata.prism:publicationName: Colloids and Surfaces A: Physicochemical and Engineering Aspects
coredata.dc:creator: Rajput, Abhishek
Raj, Savan K.
Lebedeva, Oksana V.
Chesnokova, Alexandra N.
Raskulova, Tatiana V.
Kulshrestha, Vaibhav
coredata.dc:description: Here, we investigated the functionalized carbon dots as a possible organic filler for the sulfonated poly (ether sulfone) (SPES) based composite cation exchange membranes to improve the membrane performance. Sulfonated carbon dots (SCDs) are synthesised by simple pyrolysis of citric acid in presence of sodium 4-styrene sulfonate. Functionalized carbon dots provide excellent hydrophilicity, and affinity towards SPES due to the intra hydrogen bonding between sulfonic group of SPES and functionalized carbon dot. Addition of filler material leads to the improvement in physicochemical and electrochemical properties of the composite membranes. 32 % increment in ionic conductivity is noted after the addition of 2% SCDs for SMQ-2 membrane while ion exchange capacity goesets double. Membranes are evaluated for salt removal from brackish water by electrodialysis and found that SMQ-2 membrane showed 30 % reduction in the power consumption than the SM membrane. Composite membranes can efficiently be used for the salt removal from brackish water.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Composite membranes
Ionic conductivity
Salt removal
Membrane stability

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Sulfonation of carbon dots (CDs) and PES and their membrane preparation
Chemical, structural, thermal and mechanical characterization
Characterization of SM and SMQ-X composite membrane
Water uptake (WU), swelling ratio (SW) and ion exchange capacity (IEC)
Thermal and mechanical stability of membranes
Water desalination efficiency of prepared CEM by electrodialysis

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: FTIR spectra of CDs, PSS and SCDs.
TEM images of CDs at different magnifications (size of bar 50 nm, 5 nm and 2 nm left to right).
Optical image of membranes a) SM b) SMQ-0.5 c) SMQ-1 d) SMQ- 2.
Digital photograph of SCDs incorporated membrane in normal light (A) UV light of 365 nm (B). Inset show the SCDs in normal and UV light respectively.
SEM image of surface (A), cross sectional view (B) and EDX analysis for SMQ-2 membrane.
Ionic conductivity for composite membranes with varied temperature.
Stress- strain curve for composite membranes.
Salt reduction (a) reduction in ionic conductivity (b) with time during electrodialysis with different prepared membranes.
Synthesis of functionalized carbon dots and preparation of composite membrane.
Schematic representation of electro-dialysis system for water desalination.
Membrane thickness, water uptake (WU), swelling ratio (SR), Ion exchange capacity (IEC), and ionic conductivity for composite membranes.
Mechanical strength (Elastic modulus, Stress and Strain) for composite membranes.
Desalination performance in term of efficiency and power consumption for composite membranes.
Comparison table for the ionic conductivity and desalination performance for the membranes with reported.

head.ce:title: Functionalized carbon dots composite cation exchange membranes: Improved electrochemical performance and salt removal efficiency

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Council of Scientific & Industrial Research -Central Salt and Marine Chemicals Research Institute

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Here, we investigated the functionalized carbon dots as a possible organic filler for the sulfonated poly (ether sulfone) (SPES) based composite cation exchange membranes to improve the membrane performance. Sulfonated carbon dots (SCDs) are synthesised by simple pyrolysis of citric acid in presence of sodium 4-styrene sulfonate. Functionalized carbon dots provide excellent hydrophilicity, and affinity towards SPES due to the intra hydrogen bonding between sulfonic group of SPES and functionalized carbon dot. Addition of filler material leads to the improvement in physicochemical and electrochemical properties of the composite membranes. 32 % increment in ionic conductivity is noted after the addition of 2% SCDs for SMQ-2 membrane while ion exchange capacity goesets double. Membranes are evaluated for salt removal from brackish water by electrodialysis and found that SMQ-2 membrane showed 30 % reduction in the power consumption than the SM membrane. Composite membranes can efficiently be used for the salt removal from brackish water.

ce:section.ce:para: The rising population has led to the scarcity of the fresh potable water. Several factors are involved for the depleation of water resources; industrialization is one of them, which not only consume huge amount of water as well as contaminated the ground water sources [1]. There is high demand of water, the resources are on the verge of extinction [2]. Ion exchange membrane are widely used as a separator for various operation such as vanadium redox flow battery, water desalination, fuel cell, water electrolysis, etc [3–7]. There is a need to provide safe drinking water to each and every one, by water desalination [8]. Different technologies are used for water desalination and purification such as adsorption, reverse osmosis (RO), nanofiltration (NF), ultrafiltration (UF), microfiltration (MF), electrodialysis (ED) etc [9–12]. There are several drawbacks pertaining to above methods to be used. RO, generally discards, more than 60 % of water, which leads to the wastage of water in water stressed environment and other methods including adsorption, NF, UF, MF is not effective for continuous production of water desalination [13–17]. So here we proposed to use electrodialysis, where salts can be separated via electrodriven process with high water recovery. The other advantage of ED technique is low energy consumption, separation of salt at higher ppm, high flux, longevity and scale up of process [18–21]. Ion exchange membranes (IEMs) from which ions migrate are the main component for ED system. IEM are synthesised by different methods copolymerization of copolymers, free radical polymerization and blending it with commercial polymer followed by functionalization [22,23]. Due to the presence of functional groups membranes have good electrochemical performance but lacks with stability. Different nano materials have been used as filler in the membrane matrix, which not only improve the stability as well as boost the other membrane properties such as IEC, water uptake and ionic conductivity [24,25].

Due to environment and human health carbon based nanostructures can be used as the fillers for IEMs. Carbon nanostructures were defined based on its precursor, structure and dimension such as nano carbon (CND), carbon quantum dot (CQD), carbon dot (CD) and graphene quantum dot (GQD). Therefore, depending upon the content of structural element, these nanostructures can be coined. Carbon dots (CDs), also known as graphene/graphene oxide carbon dots contains epoxy, hydroxyl and carboxyl groups at their basal planes and surfaces [26,27]. The presence of functional group allows the solubility in the solvent which is essential for the uniform dispersion in the polymeric solutions. These functional filler can be tuned and can be interacted with the functional polymers to form ion exchange membrane as per the requirement [28]. CDs are good alternative to traditional toxic quantum dot made of metals [29]. CDs possess the characteristics features of the GO; and has lower cytotoxicity than graphene flakes [30,31]. The nanoparticles have been embedded successfully in the polymer membrane to enhance the performance for the various application. SPES/PWA composite membrane improved performance for the polymer electrolyte membrane fuel cell. SPEEK/ACNT membrane improved the performance for the PEFC due to the flexible graphitic structure [32,33]. Different types of carbon dots were used as the fillers for the membranes for the removal of toxic ions from water [34,35]. Thin film nanocomposite hollow fiber membranes with functionalized carbon quantum dots showed its efficiency for brackish water desalination [36]. Performance of alcohol dehydration by pervaporation using quantum dots modified composite membrane improves remarkably [37,38]. Highly fluorescent carbon quantum dots was used in nafion solution as proton selective hybrid membrane for direct methanol fuel cells to improve the performance [39]. Due to different advantages, there is a need to explore the potential of CDs in the ion exchange membranes for water desalination.

Here, we synthesised the functionalized carbon dot composite SPES membrane. Composite membranes have been characterized by different means for the properties and their performance in water desalination.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sulfonation of carbon dots (CDs) and PES and their membrane preparation
ce:section.ce:para: Single step synthesis was carried out to synthesised the sulfonated carbon dots (SCDs) via pyrolysis method using citric acid and poly (sodium 4-styrene sulfonate) as reported [40]. The scheme of the synthesis processes of SCQD is shown below in Scheme 1 . 4 g citric acid and 1 g poly(sodium 4-styrene sulfonate) were dissolved in deionized water and heated at 100 °C until dry powder was obtained. The obtained powder was pyrolyzed for 2 h at 200 °C under inert atmosphere in tubular furnace. Dialysis bag was used to further purify the SCDs in the as-obtained product. The obtained solution was further lyophilized to obtain the product SCDs.

Sulfonation of poly (ether sulfone) (PES) was carried out using concentrated H2SO4 at 60 °C for 6 h [41]. To prepare SPES composite membranes, SCD was dispersed in Dimethyl actetamide (DMAc) (30 mL) solution by sonication for 60 min after complete dispersion of SCD, known amount of SPES (15 % w/v) was added to the solution and stirred for 12 h at 40 °C, and obtained polymeric solution was casted on the clean glass plate using Doctors blade and dried at 60 °C. The membranes were designate as SM for plain SPES membrane and SMQ-X for composites membrane, where x represent wt % of SCD (x = 0.5, 1, and 2 wt %). The schematic representation of composite membrane preparation is presented in Scheme 1.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Chemical, structural, thermal and mechanical characterization
ce:section.ce:para: Structural and chemical characterization of the membrane were done by using Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR). UV–vis, X-Ray and fluorescent spectroscopy were used to characterized the CDs and SCDs. The size and shape of CDs was confirmed by TEM. The surface characterization was carried out by Scanning electron microscopy (SEM). Thermal degradation and stability of membranes were done on thermogravimetric analyzer (TGA). Stress/strain of membranes were measured using Zwick Roell, Z2.5 universal testing machine (UTM). Details are including in supporting information section.

Physiochemical and electrochemical characterization of the composite membranes including water uptake, swelling ratio, ion exchange capacity, and ionic conductivity were performed using different techniques [42]. Details of the characterizations are including in supporting information section.

Water desalination performance of composite membranes were tested in a ED cell of effective area of 60 cm2 [43]. Precious metal oxides (titanium-ruthenium-platinum) coated TiO2 mess of 1.0 mm thickness was obtained from Titanium Tantalum Products (TITAN, Chennai, India) were used as the electrodes. NaCl solution of known concentration (0.1 M) and volume was fed into dilute compartment (DC) and concentrate compartment (CC). Na2SO4 solution (0.02 M) was circulated through electrode wash (EW) compartments to avoid the electrode reaction. Constant voltage across the electrodes was applied through DC power supply (Aplab India, model L1285), and the resulting current was recorded. During water desalination, change in TDS, conductivity and pH of both streams were recorded in a regular time intervals during the experiment. Scheme 2 shows the schematic representation of electro-dialysis system for water desalination in recirculation mode.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of SM and SMQ-X composite membrane
ce:section.ce:para: The sulphonation of the SCDs leads to the new peak at 1189 cm−1 and 1120 cm−1 present symmetric and asymmetric vibrations of the –SO3H group of SCDs. Also SO peak appears at 674 cm−1 due to the SO stretching vibration while OH group is visible at 3143 cm-1 as depicted in Fig. 1 confirms the successful preparation of functionalized carbon dots [40]. Transmission electron microscope images of synthesised SCD are shown in Fig. 2 , which confirms the uniform size distribution and spherical shape. The average size of spherical SCDs found to be 2−5 nm. The UV–vis absorption spectroscopy of SCDs exhibit an absorption peak at 220 nm correspond to π- π * transition of aromatic CC bond, revealing the SCDs formation (Fig. S-1). A broad emission spectrum is formed at 440 nm, having excited wavelength at 260 nm as shown in Fig. S-1. SCDs powder was dried for the XRD analysis. The XRD pattern presented in Fig. S-2 shows a broad diffraction peak at 2θ ∼ 20° corresponds to the graphitic (002) plane reveals amorphous nature SCDs. The high density of functional groups with CDs are responsible for the amorphous nature of SCDs [39].

The presence of sulfonic group in all the membranes confirm by the absorption bands of sulfonic acid group around 1238 cm−1, 1151 cm−1 and 1103 cm−1 for the SO, symmetric and asymmetric vibrations of the –SO3H group as shown in Fig. 3 . Due to presence of −OH group on CDs, it has good dispersion in the aqueous solution, additionally sulphonation of CDs leads to increased dispersity due to presence of more hydrophilic group. The optical images of plain and composite membranes are shown in the Fig. 4 (a–d). The addition of SCDs in membrane lead to opaque nature of membranes. The blue emission emitted by SCDs suspension under UV light typify the successful formation of SCDs, similarly the digital image of plain and composite membranes under UV light of 365 nm wavelength is shown in Fig. 5 . Thus, confirm the successfully uniform incorporated functionalized carbon dots in membrane matrix. Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) image of SMQ-2 membrane confirms the uniform distribution of SCDs in membrane. It was observed that there was no crack and hole on the surface and cross-sectional side of membrane showing dense nature of membrane as depicted in Fig. 6 (A). EDX analysis for SMQ-2 membrane was also performed to confirm the distribution of SO3H group and shown in Fig. 6 (B). Surface morphology of SM and SMQ-2 membranes was also performed by atomic force microscopy in tapping mode and presented in Fig. S-3. The image of SM presents a smooth structure with roughness average 3.83 nm, while significant increase in the surface roughness is observed in SMQ-2 membrane (10.44 nm).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Water uptake (WU), swelling ratio (SW) and ion exchange capacity (IEC)
ce:section.ce:para: Absorbed water is very essential for ion exchange membrane as it decide the transport of ions by Grotthuss and Vehicular mechanism. The water uptake of composite membranes increases by increasing the SCD content in the membrane matrix. SM membrane shows 15 % absorbed water in membrane which increases to 19 % by the incorporation of 0.5 % of SCDs in membrane, on further addition of SCDs in the membrane matrix the absorbed water reaches to 27 % for SMQ-2 membrane which is 80 % higher than that of SM membrane (Table 1 ). The increment in absorbed water in composite membranes by addition of SCD in mainly due to the hydrophilic nature of SCDs. The swelling ratio of composite membranes decreases by addition of SCDs, improvement in swelling may be due to the hydrogen bonding between the SCDs and SEPS in membrane. The ion exchange capacity (IEC) value for composite membranes (SMQ-X) improved with increasing SCDs content, in comparison to pristine SPES membrane. The increment in IEC is mainly due to presence of more sulfonic group in the SCDs. The IEC for SMQ-2 membrane goes to double in comparison with the SM membrane (1.34 meq/g) as shown in Table 1.

Ionic conductivity of membranes depends upon the functional group present in the membrane matrix and ion connecting channels. SCDs in composite membranes provide the connectivity to the channels for ions to move in membrane matrix. The conductivity is mainly due to the vehicular type mechanics of ion transport and SCDs provide the additional path for the transport of ions. SCD nanomaterial present in the polymer matrix further enhanced the ionic conductivity of the composite membranes. Ionic conductivity of SM membrane was found to be 1.30 × 10−2 S cm-1 while 1.72 × 10−2 S cm-1 for SMQ-2 membrane. Ionic conductivity jumped 32 % after the addition of 2% SCDs for SMQ-2 membrane which create interconnecting water channels in membrane matrix for ion transport. Temperature also plays an important role for the mobility of ions in the membrane matrix. To disclose the effect of temperature on ionic conductivity of composite membranes was studied from 30 °C to 90 °C. For SMQ-2 membrane the ionic conductivity increased by three times by increasing the temperature from 30 to 90 °C as shown in Fig. 7 . As the temperature increases the conductivity of all the membranes increases, for SM membrane 2.7 times higher ionic conductivity was noted at 90 °C comparison to 30 °C.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Thermal and mechanical stability of membranes
ce:section.ce:para: Thermal and mechanical stability of the membranes were performed using TGA and UTM analysis. Fig. 8 shows TGA thermogram of SM and different composite membranes. It is clear from the figure that there is three weight loss in the thermogram, first weight loss is around 80−100 °C due to surface water present in membrane. The second weight loss occurred at 320 °C is due to the degradation of functional group present in the membrane. The third and final weight loss at 505 °C followed due to the degradation of polymer backbones of the membranes. The fillers of the membrane enhance the support for the membranes against the thermal degradation as depicted in the Fig. 8, and helps in keeping the membrane intact i.e. weight loss of composite membranes considerably reduced in comparison to the SM membrane.

Mechanical stability of composite membrane was expressed in the terms of stress and strain and presented in Fig. 9 . The hydrogen bonding between the SCDs and SPES improves the elasticity of the composite membranes. The elastic modulus of the SMQ-2 membrane changed and reaches to eight times than the SM membrane. SM membrane has low strains (4.8 %) whereas composite membrane strain gets improved; strain increases upto 55.09 % after incorporation of SCDs into membrane matrix as shown in the Table 2 .

ce:section.ce:section-title: Water desalination efficiency of prepared CEM by electrodialysis
ce:section.ce:para: Current-voltage (I–V) characteristics for the prepared composite membranes have been measured to find out the values of limiting current density and shown in Fig. S-4. The increased current density leads to the ions diffusion in solution at the membrane interface layer of the dilute channel, causing depletion of salt in the solution and vice-versa at the concentrated channel. Ohmic, plateau and non-Ohmic are three characteristic regions obtained, and correlated with concentration polarization phenomena for the composite membranes [44]. Increasing voltage leads to the increase in current, until the saturation in increment of current, specifying the limiting current condition. The concentration polarization theory is not applicable to the experiment as plateau region is not visible, as narrow flat area or small inflection point is seen. Further, increasing the voltage leads to the sudden jump of current due to the water splitting in the over limiting region. From the Fig. S-4, the 1.5–2.5 V found to be the plateau region and 2 V/cell pair was considered the applied potential for ED experiments.

Effect of salt removal with time during electrodialysis is shown in Fig. 10 (a). Ions concentration decreases linearly with time due to the depletion of ions in diluted compartment (DC). SMQ-2 membranes have good efficiency of the salt removal as compared to the other membranes, this may be happened due to the high ion exchange capacity of the SMQ-2 membrane which facilitates the movement of ion from DC to CC across the ion exchange membrane. For SPES membrane (SM) concentration of DC decreases from 6000 ppm to 2236 ppm in 180 min while for the composite membrane SMQ-2 membrane it reaches to 430 ppm in same time interval. It is clear from the results that the efficiency of ionic migration by composite membrane increases with increasing SCD content in membrane matrix. Fig. 10 (b) presents the ionic conductivity of solution with time for both the compartments (DC and CC), ionic conductivity of DC decreases with time; as it increases for CC. The concentration of DC and CC changes fast in the beginning of the experiment but slow down after 60 min. it may be due to the presence of less ions in DC.

The performance of the prepared membranes for water desalination is evaluated in term of current efficiency and power consumption and presented in the Table 3 . The power consumption for SMQ-2 membrane found to be 1.34 kW h/kg of salt removal, which is 5% lower than SMQ-1 membrane and 30 % than SM membrane. Current efficiency during desalination for SMQ-2 also found to be maximum among the membranes (67 %). The electrochemical properties are responsible for the good efficiency of the composite membranes. Overall, SMQ-2 composite membrane presents better performance among the prepared membranes and could be used for salt removal from the water. With the functionalization of CDs with the poly (sodium 4‑styrene sulfonate), the antifouling properties of membranes get enhanced additionally [45,46]. Table 4 , display the comparative study of ionic conductivity and the desalination performance of the reported membrane. The reported membrane stands out with the lower power consumption and moderate current efficiency.

SPES-SCDs composite cation exchange membranes have been synthesised successfully. Physico and electrochemical properties of composite membranes improved by the incorporation of functionalized carbon dots. The SCDs acted as bridge to interconnect the ionic channels in the membrane and provide additional sites for the transport of ions. SMQ-2 composite membrane found to be superior in term of ionic conductivity and ion exchange capacity. The salt removal efficiency for SMQ-2 membrane found to be maximum among the membranes. Also, the current efficiency of SMQ-2 was 67 % while the power consumption was 1.34 kW h/kg as compared to the SM membrane (without filler) which has the current efficiency of 47 % and power consumption of 1.92 kW h/kg during the water desalination by electrodialysis. Enhanced stability and overall performance of the composite membranes mark the importance of the SCDs as filler materials for ion exchange membranes.

Abhishek Rajput: Methodology, Investigation, Writing of first draft. Savan K. Raj: Methodology, Investigation. Oksana V. Lebedeva: Formal analysis, Investigation. Alexandra N. Chesnokova: Validation, Formal analysis. Tatiana V. Raskulova: Supervision. Vaibhav Kulshrestha: Conceptualization, Writing - review & editing, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors report no declarations of interest.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Authors are thankful to Department of Science and Technology, India and Russian Foundation for Basic Research, Russia for providing financial support. Analytical and Environmental Science Division and Centralized Instrument Facility, CSIR-CSMCRI, Bhavnagar is greatly acknowledged for instrumental support.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.colsurfa.2020.125677.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:

Yoo, Sulfonated fluorinated multi-block copolymer hybrid containing sulfonated(poly ether ether ketone) and graphene oxide: A ternary hybrid membrane architecture for electrolyte applications in proton exchange membrane fuel cells, J.
